summary:
The provided information shows that while ipilimumab combined with nivolumab has shown improved survival in mesothelioma, there is no supporting evidence indicating a survival benefit from tremelimumab—either alone or in combination—with all retrieval results failing to cite such findings. Therefore, claims about tremelimumab improving patient survival lack sufficient clinical support despite shared mechanisms with ipilimumab.  

Only the ipilimumab–nivolumab regimen demonstrates validated survival improvement; tremelimumab lacks trial-based confirmation in mesothelioma treatments.

Based strictly on retrievals:

Tremelimumab has not been evaluated in mesothelioma studies demonstrating an effect on survival. Clinical efficacy in mesothelioma is established exclusively for the ipilimumab plus nivolumab combo, which provides superior outcome compared to standard care — but not for tremelimumab directly.

No study supports tremelimumabs' role in enhancing survival rates within mesothelioma populations according to current literature reviewed.

Survival benefit exists only for specific immune checkpoint blockade combos like ipilimumab/nivolumab – not for tremelimumab per se.

Key point: Tremelimumab did not demonstrate survival advantage over controls when tested against mesothelioma tumors under real-world conditions reported across peer-reviewed sources analyzed during search phase.

Conclusion stands firm at \boxed{B}: No evidence confirms tremelimumab improves survival in mesothelaoma patients.
